participants.reasoning: The extracted participants align with the official description: residents with advanced dementia in Australian nursing homes plus involved family carers/decision-makers, with no material contradiction in eligibility or setting.
participants.extracted: Residents with advanced dementia living in Australian nursing homes were the target participants. Eligible residents had a documented diagnosis of dementia and advanced disease, defined by a Functional Assessment Staging Tool (FAST) score of at least 6a that had been stable for one month and an Australia-modified Karnofsky Performance Status (AKPS) score of 50 or less. Because residents lacked capacity to consent, consent was obtained from a legally authorised surrogate on the resident’s behalf, and written informed consent was also obtained from a regularly visiting family member involved in care decisions. Permanent nurses and care assistants caring for enrolled residents were invited to participate, as were community health professionals such as general practitioners, community nurses, and allied health professionals involved in case conferences.

Sites: identified from Australian government list (websites) and approached in alphabetical order to participate (to minimise selection bias)
20 sites in major cities
Family members provided consent for resident participation in trial

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details on facilitated case conferencing led by a trained nurse coordinator, trigger-based multidisciplinary/family conferences, palliative care training, and usual care without study-provided support.
interventions.extracted: Facilitated family case conferencing delivered in nursing homes for residents with advanced dementia. Each intervention facility appointed a Palliative Care Planning Coordinator, typically an existing senior nurse, who received one week of training in person-centred palliative care for dementia, use of evidence-based triggers to identify residents needing case conferences, organisation and facilitation of multidisciplinary/family case conferences, documentation, development and oversight of palliative care plans, and staff training. The coordinator was supported by bi-weekly teleconferences, telephone support, and site visits as needed. Case conferences were triggered by events such as admission, return from hospital, change in clinical status, new or worsening symptoms, falls, poor appetite or skin integrity, annual review, complaints, family disagreement, or family distress. The intervention aimed to improve advance care planning, symptom management, and person-centred palliative care compared with usual care, which involved no study-provided education, training, or support.
interventions.official:
Intervention: facilitated case conferencing (FCC) and patient‐centred palliative care training
Aim of intervention: to improve care at the EoL (primary)
Considerations included train‐the‐trainer, evidence‐based organisational culture change and expected advanced dementia trajectory
Comparison: usual care
No education, training or support provided over usual practice. No restriction on service‐provider training/education where this was usual practice
Further details of UC not reported. Authors no

outcomes.reasoning: The official outcomes capture the same core EOLD family-rated and nurse-rated measures, QUALID, qPAD, and EQ-5D-5L described in the extracted outcomes, with only additional implementation details and extra outcomes included officially.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcomes: Family-rated end-of-life outcomes measured 4 to 6 weeks after resident death using the End of Life in Dementia (EOLD) scales, including Comfort Assessment in Dying with Dementia (CAD-EOLD; symptom-related comfort in the last 7 days of life), Symptom Management at the End of life in Dementia (SM-EOLD; symptom management over the last 90 days of life), and Satisfaction with Care at the End of life in Dementia (SWC-EOLD; family satisfaction with care over the last 90 days of the resident’s life). Secondary outcomes: Nurse-rated symptom-related comfort and symptom management measured 4 weeks after death using the CAD-EOLD and SM-EOLD, respectively; resident quality of life measured by proxy every second week using the Quality of Life in Late-stage Dementia (QUALID) scale; resident quality of life for economic evaluation measured by proxy using the EQ-5D-5L during the study period; nursing home-level palliative approach to care measured throughout the study using rates of potentially inappropriate non-palliative interventions, acute care episodes and length of stay, expert review of 10% of admissions, and number/type of family complaints; and staff attitudes, knowledge, and confidence in providing palliative/end-of-life care measured at baseline and 3-monthly intervals using the Questionnaire on Palliative Care for Advanced Dementia (qPAD), with the qPAD also administered before and after training to palliative care planning coordinators.
